items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: What is the difference between outsourcing and offshoring?
items.correct: Outsourcing means contracting work to another company; offshoring means relocating work to another country. A firm can outsource without offshoring, or offshore without outsourcing (in-house overseas).
items.misconception: Outsourcing and offshoring are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Outsourcing means moving to another country and offshoring means hiring contractors.
items.why: The distinction matters for analysis: outsourcing is about who does the work (firm boundary); offshoring is about where (geography). Many real cases are both — e.g. UK firms outsourcing call centres to Indian contractors.

items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.
The answer examiners want is: 1.9 billion. “190 million” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Nike outsources what percentage of production?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 100 percent. “50 percent” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate number of Nike production countries:
items.why: Exact answer: 41. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
